levels.body: The meaning of the word “Lover” can be divided into two denotations. The first is a benign one: “A person who is in love with, or who is enamoured of, another person” (“Lover, *n*.” 2.3.a). The second one alludes to an adulterous kind of relationship, which is negatively connotated: “A person who engages in a romantic or sexual relationship outside marriage, esp. one which is clandestine or illicit” (“Lover, *n*” 2.3.b).
worksCited: "lover, *n*. 2" *OED Online*, Oxford University Press, June 2022, www.oed.com/view/Entry/110603. Accessed 14 July 2022.

levels.body: It is likely that the poem’s title refers to the second denotation of an illicit affair. One hint for this reading is the indication that the speaker cannot spend much time with Porphyria, see lines 21-30. Porphyria chooses a secret rendezvous with the speaker over a feast which she is expected to attend (cf. lines 26-30). This proves her love for him, according to the speaker. In fact, the speaker’s conclusion that Porphyria loves him because of her skipping the feast emphasizes the illegitimate nature of their relationship. Porphyria’s visit would have no particular significance in a legitimate romantic relationship.
